messages.content:
Explain what a database index is and when adding one is the wrong call — twice,
for two different audiences:

1. **A junior developer** who knows SQL basics but has never thought about
   query performance. (~150 words)
2. **A CFO** deciding whether to approve two weeks of engineering time for
   "database performance work". No jargon. (~150 words)

The two explanations must be genuinely different in framing, not the same text
simplified. Label each section clearly.

text:
### 1. For the Junior Developer

Think of an index like the glossary at the back of a textbook. Instead of scanning every page to find `JOIN`, you flip directly to it. In SQL, an index is a hidden data structure that sorts your column values so queries can skip straight to matching rows instead of reading the whole table.

But indexes aren’t free. Every `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE` must also rewrite the index, which slows writes and eats disk space. Adding one to a tiny table (a few hundred rows) is pointless—the database scans it faster than it looks up the index. Likewise, slapping an index on a column with only two values (like `is_active`) rarely helps, because the query planner still has to read most of the table anyway. Only add indexes where reads dominate writes and the column actually filters down to a small slice of rows.

### 2. For the CFO

Imagine your team’s filing cabinet. An index is like labeling every folder so staff can grab the right document in seconds instead of pulling out every single one. It makes customer reports, order lookups, and dashboards load instantly.

But those labels cost time to create and update. If you approve two weeks of engineering work for a system that barely gets used, or for data that changes constantly, your team will spend more time maintaining the labels than saving on searches. It’s also a poor investment when the “cabinet” is already small enough to flip through quickly, or when most requests ask for broad summaries rather than specific records. Before signing off, ask: are we solving a real bottleneck that costs us customer trust or missed deadlines, or just chasing speed on something that works fine today?
